content.verdict: The Cove Eleuthera occupies a dramatic 40-acre peninsula on Eleuthera's Atlantic coast, with two private coves on either side — one calm and protected, one with rolling Atlantic waves. The property's clifftop pool, adults-only quiet zones, and proximity to Eleuthera's famous Glass Window Bridge and pink-sand beaches make it the most polished resort experience on this long, narrow Out Island.
content.best_room: The Cove's Suites are spread across a series of low-rise buildings stepping down toward the water. Cliffside Suites offer dramatic Atlantic views from private terraces; Cove-front Cottages sit directly above the calmer protected cove and give honeymooners the best swimming access. The Two-Bedroom Villa with private plunge pool is the splurge option. Honeymooners should specifically request a Cove-front Suite with a hammock terrace.

itinerary_7_nights.title: Arrival in Eleuthera
itinerary_7_nights.description: Fly into North Eleuthera or Governor's Harbour airport — both are connected to Nassau by 30-minute hops. The Cove is about 30 minutes from North Eleuthera by car. Check in, walk the property, swim in the protected cove, and have the first dinner at Point House — the Cove's fine-dining restaurant on a clifftop with both coves visible from the windows.

itinerary_7_nights.title: Glass Window Bridge
itinerary_7_nights.description: The Glass Window Bridge is one of the Bahamas' most photographed landmarks — a 30-foot-wide rocky strip where the deep navy Atlantic Ocean meets the calm turquoise Caribbean Sea, separated by a single ribbon of road. A morning visit captures the dramatic color contrast. Continue north to Preacher's Cave, where 17th-century English Puritans first sheltered after their shipwreck.

itinerary_7_nights.title: Lighthouse Beach
itinerary_7_nights.description: Lighthouse Beach at Eleuthera's southern tip is consistently rated one of the world's most beautiful beaches — a long pink-sand crescent backed by dunes, almost always empty due to the rough access road. The drive (about 90 minutes from the Cove) requires a 4WD; the Cove can arrange. Spend the day on the deserted sand with a packed lunch.

itinerary_7_nights.title: Spa & Adults Pool Day
itinerary_7_nights.description: Morning at the spa — the Cove's treatment rooms include couples cabanas overlooking the Atlantic cove. Afternoon at the adults-only Sky Pool, set on the clifftop with panoramic ocean views. Lunch at the Gregory Town Café back at the property, sunset cocktails on Atlantic Cove deck as the waves roll in.

itinerary_7_nights.title: Harbour Island Day Trip
itinerary_7_nights.description: A 25-minute drive to North Eleuthera and a quick water taxi reaches Harbour Island — the most charming small island in the Bahamas, with golf-cart traffic, pastel clapboard cottages, and the famous three-mile Pink Sands Beach. Lunch at Sip Sip on the cliff, afternoon walk on the pink sand, return to the Cove by sunset.

itinerary_7_nights.title: Snorkeling & Gregory Town
itinerary_7_nights.description: Morning snorkeling at Surfer's Beach or one of the offshore reefs accessible by the Cove's boat. Afternoon visit to Gregory Town — a sleepy fishing village famous for the Pineapple Festival each June and Nor'side Surf shop. Sample the local Eleuthera pineapple, said to be the world's sweetest. Dinner at Freddie's, a beloved local spot.

itinerary_7_nights.title: Final Cove Swim & Departure
itinerary_7_nights.description: A final morning swim in the protected cove, breakfast on the terrace, and the drive back to North Eleuthera airport for the connection through Nassau. The Cove's character — the dual coves, the clifftop drama, the wild Atlantic energy — leaves a strong impression.

caveats: Eleuthera is a long, narrow island with limited infrastructure — restaurants outside the resort are casual and roads can be rough; the Atlantic side has occasional rough surf and the protected cove is the only safe swimming on property.
The Cove is family-friendly and not adults-only — high seasons see more families, though the adults-only Sky Pool and the property's scale absorb the mix well.
Travel logistics involve a connection through Nassau and a domestic transfer; total journey time from US cities is 6 to 8 hours, longer than direct-flight Bahamas alternatives like Nassau.

faqs.question: Good for honeymooners?
faqs.answer: The Cove is excellent for honeymooners who want a polished resort experience on an Out Island — easier than fully off-grid Andros or Exumas but still genuinely quiet, with day-trip access to Harbour Island's pink sands and Eleuthera's spectacular Glass Window Bridge. The clifftop pool and Atlantic Cove dining make it dramatically scenic.

faqs.question: Best time to visit?
faqs.answer: November through May is the dry season. December through April offers peak weather. The Atlantic side can have winter swells (great for surfing at nearby Surfer's Beach, less so for swimming) — protected cove always offers calm water. Hurricane season runs August through October.

faqs.question: Book in advance?
faqs.answer: The Cove sells out 3 to 5 months in advance for high season. Cove-front Cottages and the Two-Bedroom Villa with plunge pool book first. Christmas, New Year, and February break require early reservations.

faqs.question: Adults-only?
faqs.answer: The Cove is not adults-only but has dedicated adult zones — the Sky Pool is the main adults-only space and the property's scale means honeymooners can find quiet. Mid-week and shoulder season are markedly quieter than holiday weeks.

faqs.question: Best room type?
faqs.answer: Cove-front Suites with hammock terraces overlook the protected cove — best swimming access and the most romantic position. Cliffside Suites offer dramatic Atlantic drama. Honeymooners seeking maximum privacy should consider the Two-Bedroom Villa with private plunge pool.

faqs.question: How to get there?
faqs.answer: North Eleuthera airport is 30 minutes by car from the Cove; Governor's Harbour is about 90 minutes. Both are 30-minute flights from Nassau. The Cove arranges transfers. Direct flights from Florida cities (Fort Lauderdale, Miami) reach North Eleuthera seasonally.
